rulings.Case Title: KEN ELDER V. U.S. BANK, N.A.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Approval Of (1) Settlement Agreement And Dismissal Of Action; And (2) Relator'S Application For Relator'S Fee, And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 1, PLAINTIFF KEN ELDER'S Motion For Approval Of (1) Settlement Agreement And Dismissal Of Action; And (2) Relator'S Application For Relator'S Fee Continued to February 10, 2025. The moving party is ordered to provide courtesy copies forthwith in compliance with SF Local Rule 2.7B. Further failures to follow the local rule regarding courtesy copies may result in an order to show cause re sanctions. Please attach a cover letter reflecting the new hearing date. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO VS. RECOLOGY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calender for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 2, 1 - DEFENDANT MYDATT SERVICES, INC'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.'s motion to compel further responses to Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests for Production of Documents, Set 1 is GRANTED. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Mydatt has shown Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete. In these responses, DiGiacomo merely provides his objections and states he will nonetheless "comply with the demand" by listing documents to requests for which no objection is being made. As DiGiacomo objects to every response, this statement essentially means he will not identify any documents. Moreover, Mydatt represents that DiGiacomo has only provided one document, a fee waiver, in response to these requests. DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete. Next, DiGiacomo has not demonstrated that any of his objections have merit. He claims his sole asset is exempt as Supplemental Security Income (SSI), thus these requests are not likely to lead to discovery of admissible evidence. But whether his assets are ultimately exempt from levy is distinct from whether the requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court orders DiGiacomo to provide further responses to "post-judgment discovery, requests for production of documents" set 1 Nos. 1 through 62. Mydatt's meet and confer was reasonable. The court does not find DiGiacomo acted with substantial justification. DiGiacomo shall pay $1,125 to Mydatt in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO VS. RECOLOGY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calender Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 2, 2 - DEFENDANT MYDATT SERVICES, INC'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.'s motion to compel further responses to post-judgment special interrogatories, set 1 is GRANTED. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Mydatt has shown Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete. For instance, special interrogatories No. 2 asks DiGiacomo to state the amount of his rent. DiGiacomo's response, i.e., that his sole assets are currently limited to disability payments, does not answer this question. Next, DiGiacomo has not demonstrated that any of his objections have merit. He claims his sole asset is exempt as Supplemental Security Income (SSI), thus no answers he provides would leave to discovery of admissible evidence. (Oppo, 4.) But whether his assets are ultimately exempt from levy is distinct from whether the requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court orders DiGiacomo to provide further responses to "post-judgment discovery, special interrogatories" set 1 Nos. 1 through 25. Mydatt's meet and confer was reasonable. The court does not find DiGiacomo acted with substantial justification. DiGiacomo shall pay $1,575 to Mydatt in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

The court is inclined as set out below. Defendant Pro Energy Services Group, LLC failed to serve timely responses to Plaintiffs' requests for production of documents. After Plaintiffs noticed a hearing for an order compelling responses and sanctions, Pro Energy served responses. Pro Energy has waived objections by failing to serve timely responses. rulings.Case Title: CHIA WEI HSU VS. DOORDASH, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 3, DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant's demurrer to the second amended complaint ("SAC") is sustained with 20 days' leave to amend. (CCP 430.10(e).) The court rejects defendant's statute of limitations argument. Defendant argues that plaintiff's claims accrued on the date plaintiff last rendered a service and citing paragraph 15 of the SAC, that date was May 2, 2020. (E.O.C. Ord, Inc. v. Kovakovich (1988) 200 Cal.App.3d 1194, 1203.) This argument fails for two reasons. First, paragraph 15 of the SAC alleges that plaintiff executed the assignment on May 2, 2020. It is unclear whether that was the last date of service. Second, plaintiff timely filed the original complaint based on Emergency Rule 9(a): "Notwithstanding any other law, the statutes of limitations and repose for civil cause of action that exceed 180 days are tolled from April 6, 2020, until October 1, 2020." Emergency Rule 9 tolled plaintiff's statute of limitations from May 2, 2020, through October 1, 2020. Thus, plaintiff timely filed the original complaint on June 9, 2022. Next, defendant relies on the Proprietary Information and Inventions Agreement ("PIIA") to argue that this express agreement bars plaintiff's claims for unjust enrichment and quantum meruit. (Sepanoissian v. National Ready Mixed Concrete Co. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 192, 207 [unjust enrichment inapplicable where the parties had an enforceable express contract]; Hedging Concepts, Inc. v. First All. Mortg. Co. (1996) 41 Cal.App.4th 1410, 1419 ["[I]t is well settled that there is no equitable basis for an implied-in-law promise to pay reasonable value when the parties have an actual agreement covering compensation."].) The court will take judicial notice of this agreement, which is attached to the declaration of Justin Steffen filed on August 9, 2022. Plaintiff quotes from the PIIA and does not dispute its authenticity. (Evidence Code section 452(h) [facts not subject to dispute]; Scott v. JPMorgan Chase Bank (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 743, 746 ["[W]e conclude that the trial court properly took judicial notice of the fact and legal effect of the government's contract with JPMorgan, since Scott did not allege or argue in the trial court that the contract was inauthentic or otherwise reasonably subject to dispute."]; Ingram v. Flippo (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1280, 1285, fn.3 [taking judicial notice of a letter that was not attached to the complaint where both parties referred to and quoted from it] (disapproved on other grounds in Leon v. County of Riverside (2023) 14 Cal.5th 910.) Plaintiff's opposition concedes that the PIIA is enforceable and governs his entitlement to payment. (Opp. Br. 5-6.) In any event the PIIA in Paragraph 8 contemplates that plaintiff's obligations survive the termination of his employment. Plaintiff's current-pleaded claims therefore fail. (See Lance Camper Manufacturing Corp. v. Republic Indemnity Co. (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 194, 203 ["The Insured must allege that the express contract is void or was rescinded in order to proceed with its quasi-contract claim."].) (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK HUYNH VS. EDWARD OCHI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 7, DEFENDANTS EDWARD OCHI AN INDIVIDUAL, CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Continued to February 10, 2025. Opposing party is ordered to provide courtesy copies forthwith in compliance with SF Local Rule 2.7B. Further failures to follow the local rule regarding courtesy copies may result in an order to show cause re sanctions. Please attach a cover letter reflecting the new hearing date. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID COTA VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY, A DELWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF DAVID COTA'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff David Cota's motion to compel further responses to Plaintiff's first set of RFPs is granted in part and denied in part. With respect to request nos. 1-11 and 13-14, these requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP 2017.010.) Cota has satisfied his burden of setting forth specific facts showing good cause for production. (CCP 2031.310(b)(1).) These requests relate to the pickup truck at issue in this case. As a general matter, where Ford responds with objections and then a statement of selected documents it will produce, it does not comply with its obligations under CCP 2031.240 to identify documents that are the subject of the objection and withheld on that basis. With respect to request 12, Ford's response was adequate and no further response is ordered. With respect to request nos. 15-31, these requests relate to Cota's allegation that Ford's violation of the Song-Beverly Act was willful and that Cota is entitled to civil penalties. The relevance of documents produced depends on Cota's showing that Ford violated the Song-Beverly Act. Ford objected on breadth and burdens grounds but offered to produce specified documents and categories of documents. In a supplemental response that postdated Cota's motion to compel, Ford offered to produce documents related to other customers' complaints about the same model of truck that Cota owned. Cota's motion to compel makes broad arguments about the scope of potentially admissible material in a Song-Beverly case but does not set forth specific facts justifying further production of documents with reference to, for example, what is missing from the documents Ford has already produced or offered to produce. Cota has not satisfied his burden at this juncture in the case of setting forth specific facts showing good cause for production. (CCP 2031.310(b)(1).) The motion is denied as to these requests without prejudice to Cota's service of RFPs concerning these topics after Ford has completed its initial production. Ford is ordered to amend its responses to request nos 1-11 and 13-14 within 15 days of the date of this order. Ford shall provide a verification. Within 30 days of the date of this order, Ford is ordered to complete its production of all responsive documents, and any documents produced in response to request nos. 15-31, other than documents withheld on grounds of privilege, for which a privilege log shall be produced. The court finds that Cota's meet and confer efforts were reasonable. (end of part 1 tenttive ruling, see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, January 31, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF DAVID COTA'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)
